questions.question: Which statement best characterizes React's core design philosophy?
options: It is declarative: you describe the UI as a function of state and React reconciles the screen to match
It is imperative: you hand-write each DOM mutation in response to events as the page changes
It is a full framework prescribing routing, dependency injection, and HTTP out of the box
It is a CSS-in-JS styling engine that bundles an optional component layer on top
questions.explanation: React is declarative: you describe the target UI as a function of state and React derives the DOM operations. The imperative-mutation description is the opposite of React's model, and React is a library, not a batteries-included framework.

questions.question: In a browser app targeting React 18+/19, which API mounts the React root into a DOM node?
options: `createRoot` from `react-dom/client`
`render` from the `react` core package
`mount` from the `react-native` package
`hydrate` from `react-dom/server`
questions.explanation: The browser renderer is `react-dom`, and you mount the concurrent root with `createRoot` from `react-dom/client`. React core is platform-agnostic and has no DOM-mounting API of its own.

questions.question: React is best described as a framework because it ships routing, forms, HTTP, and dependency injection in the box.
questions.explanation: React is a library focused on the view/component layer; routing, forms, data fetching, and similar concerns are deliberately "bring your own" via companion libraries. The in-box, opinionated description fits a framework like Angular.

questions.question: React's core component model is platform-agnostic, with pluggable renderers targeting hosts such as the browser DOM, native mobile, and the server.
questions.explanation: React core is platform-agnostic; a separate renderer targets each host (`react-dom` for the browser, React Native for mobile, server rendering, and even custom renderers).

questions.question: Which app concern does React itself provide built in, rather than requiring a companion library?
options: Component state and effects
URL-to-view routing and nested routes
Server-state caching and request deduplication
Schema-based form validation and submission
questions.explanation: React provides the component model with built-in state and effects. Routing, data-fetching/server-state caching, and form validation are each external concerns handled by companion libraries like React Router, TanStack Query, and React Hook Form.

questions.question: How do meta-frameworks like Next.js and React Router relate to React itself?
options: They wrap React with routing, data fetching, and server rendering, while React stays a library
They are forks of React core that swap the reconciler for their own diffing engine
They are required before any React component can be rendered to the DOM
They convert React into a low-level imperative DOM-scripting toolkit
questions.explanation: Meta-frameworks layer routing, data fetching, and server rendering on top of React to deliver a framework-like experience; React itself remains a library. They do not replace the reconciler, are not required for basic DOM rendering, and do not make React imperative.

questions.question: Why does React maintain a virtual DOM instead of mutating the real DOM directly on every change?
options: Building and diffing in-memory JS objects is cheap, so React can apply just the minimal real mutations
The virtual DOM is unconditionally faster than any hand-written DOM update in every possible case
The browser is unable to mutate the real DOM without a virtual representation backing it
The virtual DOM removes layout, reflow, and repaint from the rendering pipeline entirely
questions.explanation: Comparing plain JS objects is cheap relative to real DOM work, letting React commit only the minimal mutations. It is not unconditionally faster than every hand-tuned update, the browser needs no virtual DOM, and reflow/repaint still occur when the real DOM actually changes.

questions.question: What is the correct order of the phases React runs in response to a state change?
options: Render (build new virtual tree) → Reconcile (diff against previous tree) → Commit (apply DOM mutations)
Commit (apply DOM mutations) → Render (build virtual tree) → Reconcile (diff the trees)
Reconcile (diff the trees) → Render (build virtual tree) → Commit (apply DOM mutations)
Render (build virtual tree) → Commit (apply DOM mutations) → Reconcile (diff the trees)
questions.explanation: A state change triggers Render (produce a new virtual tree), then Reconcile (diff new vs. retained previous tree), then Commit (apply only the minimal real-DOM mutations the browser must paint). The diff cannot run before the new tree exists, and committing precedes nothing in this cycle.

questions.question: In React 18+, multiple state updates triggered inside a promise, setTimeout, or native event handler are automatically batched into a single coordinated re-render.
questions.explanation: React 18+ extends automatic batching beyond React event handlers to include updates inside promises, timeouts, and native event handlers, so multiple updates yield one coordinated re-render and commit.

questions.question: During reconciliation, what happens when an element keeps the **same type** across renders but only a prop changes, e.g. `<div className="a">` becomes `<div className="b">`?
options: React keeps the existing DOM node and patches only the changed attribute, with no remount
React tears down the existing node and builds a fresh one, destroying any child state
React ignores the change because `className` is not included in the diff comparison
React clones the node and renders both the old and new versions side by side
questions.explanation: Same element type means update in place: React reuses the existing DOM node and patches only the changed attribute. A teardown/remount happens only when the element type itself changes.

questions.question: Which statements about React's reconciliation heuristics are correct? (Select all that apply.)
options: A general optimal tree diff is O(n³), so React uses O(n) heuristics that hold for real UIs
Elements of the same type are updated in place rather than remounted
Changing an element from one type to another tears down the old subtree and destroys its state
React's diff is guaranteed to find the globally minimal set of edits between any two trees
Array index keys are always the safest choice for list items
questions.explanation: React trades the O(n³) optimal tree diff for O(n) heuristics: same-type elements update in place, different-type elements remount and lose state. It does not guarantee a globally minimal edit set, and index keys cause subtle bugs when lists reorder.

questions.question: Why is using the array index as a `key` for a reorderable list considered a bug-prone choice?
options: When items reorder, the index stays put while items move, so React reuses nodes for the wrong rows
Index keys throw a runtime error in React 19 whenever a list is reordered or filtered
Index keys force every single list item to fully remount on each and every render
Index keys turn off reconciliation for the entire list, so nothing in it ever updates
questions.explanation: Keys identify siblings across renders; an index key stays the same while the underlying item moves, so React reuses nodes for the wrong items and state can attach to the wrong row. It is not a thrown error, nor does it force full remounts or disable reconciliation.

questions.question: What is the purpose of React's `StrictMode` double-invoking renders and re-running effect setup/cleanup in development?
options: To surface impure renders and missing effect cleanup; it has no effect in production
To deliberately make production renders run twice as a runtime safety margin
To permanently disable component memoization across the whole component tree
To batch and deduplicate outgoing network requests issued across components
questions.explanation: In development StrictMode double-invokes renders and mounts/unmounts/re-mounts effects to expose impure renders and missing cleanup. It is a development-only aid and has no effect in production builds.

questions.question: A slot conditionally renders `<ProfileA />` or `<ProfileB />` (two different component types) in the same position. A user toggles back and forth. What happens to the component's internal state on each toggle?
options: State is reset each toggle, because swapping the type tears down the old subtree and mounts fresh
State is preserved across every toggle, because both components occupy the same DOM slot
State is shared between `ProfileA` and `ProfileB` since they render into one position
Only `ProfileA`'s state persists across toggles, while `ProfileB` starts fresh each time
questions.explanation: Different element type at the same position is a remount: React destroys the old subtree (and its state) and builds the new type fresh, so each toggle resets state. Sharing position does not preserve or share state across distinct component types.

questions.question:
Replacing `
{content}
` with `{content}` at the same position triggers which effects? (Select all that apply.)
options: The old `<div>` subtree is torn down and a new `<span>` subtree is built
Any component state held within that subtree is destroyed
React patches the tag name in place and preserves child state
Child component effects in the old subtree run their cleanup, and the new subtree's effects run setup
questions.explanation: Different element type forces a remount: the old subtree (and its state) is destroyed, its effects clean up, and the new subtree mounts and runs setup. React cannot patch a tag name in place because the types differ.

questions.question: Because StrictMode may invoke a component's render function twice in development, a render that mutates external state or logs a side effect can produce visibly different behavior under StrictMode than without it.
questions.explanation: Renders must be pure functions of props and state. StrictMode's intentional double-invocation exposes exactly these impurities: a render with side effects will run them twice, making the impurity visible — which is the diagnostic point of the behavior.

questions.question: Across the render → reconcile → commit cycle, which stage is the comparatively expensive one and why?
options: Commit, because it touches the real DOM, which is costly relative to building and diffing JS objects
Render, because constructing the JS objects for the virtual tree dominates the overall cost
Reconcile, because running the O(n) diff is more expensive than mutating the real DOM
All three stages cost roughly the same, since each one only operates on the virtual tree
questions.explanation: Render (building JS objects) and reconcile (an O(n) diff) are cheap; the commit stage is comparatively expensive because it mutates the real DOM. Reconcile operates on virtual trees, and commit is the only stage that touches the real DOM.

questions.question: React deliberately does **not** compute a provably optimal diff between two virtual trees. What is the senior-level justification?
options: An optimal general tree diff is O(n³); O(n) heuristics are near-optimal for how real UIs change
An optimal tree diff is simply impossible to implement in JavaScript at any reasonable scale
An optimal diff would require mutating the real DOM during the reconcile phase to stay correct
Heuristic diffing runs only in development and is replaced by an optimal diff in production builds
questions.explanation: The optimal general tree diff is O(n³), impractical at UI scale, so React uses O(n) heuristics (same-type-in-place, key-based sibling matching) that are near-optimal for real UI mutations. Optimal diffing is computable but too slow; the heuristics run in both dev and production, and reconcile never touches the real DOM.
